data.reasoningText:
The user has a skeleton .NET 10 project with some packages already referenced. Let me build the research agent. The packages are:
- `Microsoft.Agents.AI` - for agent functionality
- `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAI` - for OpenAI integration
- `OpenAI` - OpenAI SDK

Let me look at what `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` provides. This is the new Microsoft.Extensions.AI abstraction layer. I should use `IChatClient` and the function-calling/tool infrastructure.

Actually, looking at the packages more carefully:
- `Microsoft.Agents.AI` version 1.*-* - this is pre-release


- `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAI` version 9.* - the AI abstractions for OpenAI
- `OpenAI` version 2.* - the OpenAI .NET SDK

The Microsoft.Extensions.AI framework provides `IChatClient`, `ChatMessage`, `AIFunction`, and tool-calling capabilities. I'll build a research agent by first creating an OpenAI chat client, then defining tools for web search and note-taking, and finally implementing the agent loop that orchestrates these capabilities.

My approach leverages `FunctionInvokingChatClient` middleware to handle tool calls automatically. I'll need a web search tool (using a real HTTP call or simulated) and a simple in-memory note store for capturing findings. Let me start writing the Program.cs using Microsoft.Extensions.AI patterns.

Writing the agent setup... I need to structure this with a static `ResearchTools` class that holds the web search and note-taking methods, then wire them up in the main program using `AIFunctionFactory`. For the web search implementation, I'll use environment variables for the OpenAI API key and handle web search through a simple HTTP approach, possibly leveraging a free search interface or documenting the API requirements.

Setting up the code now with configuration for the OpenAI model and API key, an in-memory notebook for storing notes, and building out the tool definitions... Setting up the system prompt that instructs the agent to search multiple times with different queries, take notes on findings, and synthesize everything into a final summary. Then I'm getting the research topic from the user and initializing the chat messages with the system instructions and the user's research request before sending it to the client.

Now I'm handling the response output and implementing the web search function that takes a query, makes an HTTP request to DuckDuckGo, and parses the HTML results...

Actually, I need to double-check a few things about the API surface here—specifically whether `AIFunctionFactory.Create` accepts a delegate directly, how the function invocation pattern works with the client builder, and whether the current version of `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` uses `GetResponseAsync` or `CompleteAsync` instead. I'm settling on the API surface for Microsoft.Extensions.AI—`GetResponseAsync` with `ChatResponse` and `ChatOptions.Tools` for function calling, plus `AIFunctionFactory.Create()` and the builder pattern with `UseFunctionInvocation()` for automatic tool invocation. For the web search implementation, I'll parse DuckDuckGo's HTML directly with string manipulation to keep dependencies minimal, and I'll skip worrying about `Microsoft.Agents.AI` since it's likely tied to the Bot Framework.

Setting up the code... Setting up the chat client with function invocation middleware so the agent can call tools. Now I'm prompting the user for a research topic and setting up the agent with search, note-taking, and note-reading capabilities, along with a system prompt that instructs it to conduct thorough research through multiple web searches and documentation.

Implementing the search tool... I'm implementing functions to save and retrieve research notes, along with a helper to strip HTML tags from search results.

Now I need to add error handling around the web search HTTP calls and then build to see what the actual API surface looks like in Microsoft.Extensions.AI 9.x—the method names and return types might differ from what I'm expecting, so I'll iterate based on compiler errors rather than guessing.

data.reasoningText:
So the actual resolved versions are:
- Microsoft.Agents.AI 1.2.0
- Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAI 10.3.0 (even though the version spec is 9.*, it resolved to 10.3.0 - actually that's odd, 9.* should not resolve to 10.x. Let me check again.)

Wait, actually `9.*` might resolve to `9.x` and the output might be showing it differently. Let me look at the output format more carefully. The `dotnet list package` output shows `[Requested, Resolved]` columns. So `9.*` resolved to `10.3.0`? That seems wrong unless the version ranges work differently.


Actually, I think I'm misunderstanding how NuGet version ranges work here. The `9.*` syntax in the csproj might be interpreted as `[9.0.0, *)` which would match any version 9.0.0 or higher, so it could resolve to 10.3.0 if that's what's available. I'm going to stop second-guessing the version mismatch and just start writing the code—the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package provides the core types I need like `IChatClient` and `ChatMessage`, so I'll rely on compiler errors to catch any API incompatibilities rather than trying to debug the versioning further.

# Microsoft.Extensions.AI

.NET developers need to integrate and interact with a growing variety of artificial intelligence (AI) services in their apps. The `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` libraries provide a unified approach for representing generative AI components, and enable seamless integration and interoperability with various AI services.

## The packages

The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions) package provides the core exchange types, including [`IChatClient`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.ichatclient) and [`IEmbeddingGenerator<TInput,TEmbedding>`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.iembeddinggenerator-2). Any .NET library that provides an LLM client can implement the `IChatClient` interface to enable seamless integration with consuming code.

The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI) package has an implicit dependency on the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions` package. This package enables you to easily integrate components such as automatic function tool invocation, telemetry, and caching into your applications using familiar dependency injection and middleware patterns. For example, it provides the [`UseOpenTelemetry(ChatClientBuilder, ILoggerFactory, String, Action<OpenTelemetryChatClient>)`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions.useopentelemetry#microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions-useopentelemetry(microsoft-extensions-ai-chatclientbuilder-microsoft-extensions-logging-iloggerfactory-system-string-system-action((microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclient)))) extension method, which adds OpenTelemetry support to the chat client pipeline.

## Which package to reference

Libraries that provide implementations of the abstractions typically reference only `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`.

To also have access to higher-level utilities for working with generative AI components, reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package instead (which itself references `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`). Most consuming applications and services should reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package along with one or more libraries that provide concrete implementations of the abstractions.

## Install the package

From the command-line:

```console
dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.AI
```

Or directly in the C# project file:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.AI" Version="[CURRENTVERSION]" />
</ItemGroup>
```

## Documentation

Refer to the [Microsoft.Extensions.AI libraries documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/microsoft-extensions-ai) for more information and API usage examples.

## Feedback & Contributing

We welcome feedback and contributions in [our GitHub repo](https://github.com/dotnet/extensions).

The `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` libraries provide a unified approach for representing generative AI components, and enable seamless integration and interoperability with various AI services.

## The packages

The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions) package provides the core exchange types, including [`IChatClient`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.ichatclient) and [`IEmbeddingGenerator<TInput,TEmbedding>`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.iembeddinggenerator-2). Any .NET library that provides an LLM client can implement the `IChatClient` interface to enable seamless integration with consuming code.

The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI) package has an implicit dependency on the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions` package. This package enables you to easily integrate components such as automatic function tool invocation, telemetry, and caching into your applications using familiar dependency injection and middleware patterns. For example, it provides the [`UseOpenTelemetry(ChatClientBuilder, ILoggerFactory, String, Action<OpenTelemetryChatClient>)`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions.useopentelemetry#microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions-useopentelemetry(microsoft-extensions-ai-chatclientbuilder-microsoft-extensions-logging-iloggerfactory-system-string-system-action((microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclient)))) extension method, which adds OpenTelemetry support to the chat client pipeline.

## Which package to reference

Libraries that provide implementations of the abstractions typically reference only `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`.

To also have access to higher-level utilities for working with generative AI components, reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package instead (which itself references `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`). Most consuming applications and services should reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package along with one or more libraries that provide concrete implementations of the abstractions.

## Install the package

From the command-line:

```console
dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.AI
```

Or directly in the C# project file:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.AI" Version="[CURRENTVERSION]" />
</ItemGroup>
```

## Documentation

Refer to the [Microsoft.Extensions.AI libraries documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/microsoft-extensions-ai) for more information and API usage examples.

## Feedback & Contributing

We welcome feedback and contributions in [our GitHub repo](https://github.com/dotnet/extensions).

data.content: Build succeeds with zero warnings. Let me verify the final code:

Builds cleanly with 0 warnings and 0 errors. Here's what the app does:

**Architecture:**
- Uses `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` with the `IChatClient` abstraction and `FunctionInvokingChatClient` middleware for automatic tool calling
- Connects to OpenAI (configurable via `OPENAI_API_KEY` and `OPENAI_MODEL` env vars, defaults to `gpt-4o-mini`)

**Three tools exposed to the agent:**
| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `SearchWebAsync` | Queries DuckDuckGo, parses HTML snippets — no API key needed |
| `TakeNote` | Saves findings to an in-memory notebook |
| `ReadNotes` | Retrieves all saved notes for synthesis |

**Agent loop:** The system prompt instructs the model to search ≥3 times with varied queries, take notes after each search, then read notes and produce a final summary. `UseFunctionInvocation()` handles the tool-call loop automatically.

**Run it:**
```bash
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
dotnet run --project ResearchAgent
```
